figures.caption: Figure 1: Generalization–memorization trade-off induced by regularization for the RAF model at fraction of facts ε = 0.1 and sample complexity α = 2.0/(1 − ε). Left: KRR, square loss. Right: SVM, hinge loss. Each plot shows the parametric curve λ 7→ (Egen(λ), Emem(λ)) for different models: a linear perceptron and kernel regression with kernels corresponding to erf and ReLU in Eq. (33), together with the Bayes-optimal generalization baseline EBO

Figure 13: Comparison between asymptotic theory for RAF data and direct kernel experiments for the spherical Gaussian kernel K(ρ) = e−η(1−ρ), for their respective optimal bandwidths ηopt. In both theory and numerics α = 2.0, ε = 0.1. For the numerics we use d = 3000 and the error bars are computed over 60 different realizations. Left panel: square loss / KRR (ηsquare
Figure 7: We show the heatmap of the Bayes-optimal test error as a function of the sample complexity α and the fraction of random labels in the training set ε.

Table 1: Representative kernels and the corresponding coefficients (µ0, µ1, µ⋆). For the RAF model with Gaussian inputs, the asymptotic errors depend on the kernel only through (µ1, µ⋆), as established in Sec. 2.2 and Appendix A.5.
Figure 8: Top panels: heatmaps of the generalization error Esquare gen (top left) and memorization error Esquare

Figure 14: CIFAR10-RAF: we compute the memorization and generalization error for KRR with α = 4.0 and ε = 0.2 for both a spherical Gaussian RBF kernel as in Eq. (220), and a polynomial kernel as in Eq. (221) with coefficients matching the Gaussian case. In all cases we average the data over 10 different realizations and the regularization spans over the interval [10−5, 102]. Left panel: we show the spans for three representative couples of the parameters µ1 and µ⋆ in the case of the quadratic kernel. Right panel: comparison between the spherical Gaussian RBF kernel and a quadratic kernel with matching coefficients µ1, µ⋆ and η = 1.589. Despite the asymptotic theory matches in the two cases, the CIFAR10-RAF dataset does not yield empirical trade-off curves analogous to those presented in Fig. 5. This contrasts with the Gaussian RAF prediction and shows that, on structured non-Gaussian data, the pair (µ1, µ⋆) is no longer a complete descriptor of kernel performance.

Figure 3: Generalization and memorization dependence on the angle γ = arctan(µ1/µ⋆). KRR (square loss) in the top panels, SVM (hinge loss) in the bottom panels. Left panels: Egen(λ → 0+) − minγ Esquare

Figure 6: The figure displays generalization errors EBO gen and Esquare gen (λopt), Ehinge gen (λopt) for γ = arctan(µ1/µ⋆) = π/4 against α in log-log scale. The fraction of facts in the training set is fixed to a representative value, which is ε = 0.5. The dashed black segment has a slope equal to −1, while the other two segments have slopes equal to −1/2. This figure illustrates the suboptimality of the generalization rate of the kernel methods in the RAF model.

Figure 11: Representative trade-off curves λ 7→ (Egen(λ), Emem(λ)) for a broad range of angles γ = arctan(µ1/µ⋆), for ε = 0.5 and α(1 − ε) = 4.0. Left: square loss. Right: hinge loss. The distinguished angles highlighted in the legend in purple correspond to the theoretical optima discussed in Sec. 3.3. For the square loss, the curve with γ = γopt
Figure 16: Left: Phase diagram as produced by AI in the conversation. Right: Generalization error at optimal regularization Esquare

Figure 9: Exponent of the power law decay of the optimal SVM generalization error Ehinge gen (λopt) at large α as a function of the angle γ = arctan(µ1/µ⋆). The fraction of the random facts is fixed to ε = 0.5. The value δ of the exponent of the power law α−1/δ is numerically estimated from a linear fit in log-log scale of the generalization error at the optimal regularization Ehinge

Figure 5: Qualitative comparison between theory for the RAF model (upper left panel) and experiments on real data, namely the CIFAR10-RAF task (lower and right panels). In all panels, we use RBF kernel ridge regression with α = 4.0 and ε = 0.2. The upper panels show the parametric memorization–generalization curves λ 7→ (Egen(λ), Emem(λ)). The kernel bandwidth η for CIFAR10-RAF is selected based on the lower panels: generalization error as a function of η at optimal regularization (left), with a minimum around η ≈ 4 (red dashed line), and at small regularization (right), with a minimum around η ≈ 6.75 (purple dashed line). The parametric curves are then shown for these two values of η, together with one larger and one smaller representative value. The RAF model exhibits qualitatively similar behavior as a function of η, with the corresponding values shifted.

Figure 2: Finite-width random features vs. kernel limit. Parametric memorizationgeneralization trade-off curves λ 7→ ( Egen(λ), Emem(λ) ) for increasing model widths κ, showing convergence to the κ → ∞ kernel prediction. Data model parameters: ε = 0.1, α(1 − ε) = 2. The endpoints are numerically obtained in the random features case.

Figure 12: Generalization and memorization dependence on the angle γ = arctan(µ1/µ⋆). KRR (square loss) in the top panels, SVM (hinge loss) in the bottom panels. Left panels: Egen(λ → 0+) − minγ Esquare

Figure 15: Qualitative comparison between SVM theory for the RAF model (upper left panel) and experiments on real data, namely the CIFAR10-RAF task (lower and right panels). In all panels, we use RBF SVM with α = 4.0 and ε = 0.2. The upper panels show the parametric memorization– generalization curves λ 7→ (Egen(λ), Emem(λ)). The kernel bandwidth η for CIFAR10-RAF is selected based on the lower panels: generalization error as a function of η at optimal regularization (left), with a minimum around η ≈ 4 (red dashed line), and at small regularization (right), with a minimum around η ≈ 7.5 (purple dashed line). The parametric curves are then shown for these two values of η, together with one larger and one smaller representative value. The curves are constructed spanning the regularization in the interval λ ∈ [10−5, 102]. For the SVM experiments, the large-regularization part of the curves can drift toward near-random-guessing performance due to finite-size effects and numerical aspects of the optimization. The RAF model exhibits qualitatively similar behavior as a function of η, with the corresponding values shifted.

Figure 4: Memorization-generalization trade-off curves λ 7→ (Egen(λ), Emem(λ)) for kernel methods in the RAF model at ε = 0.1 and α(1−ε) = 2.0. The left panel reports square loss, while the right panel reports hinge loss. Curves are shown for representative kernel geometries parameterized by the angles γ (38). For square loss (left), we display the curve for the optimal angle γopt
